coredata.dc:title: Hydrogen bonds and space restriction promoting long-lived room-temperature phosphorescence and its application for white light-emitting diodes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Miao
Lin, Liuquan
Wang, Xin
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: Achieving the long-lived and strong room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) is challengeable but desirable, especially for the enhanced phosphorescence and metal-free nanomaterials. Herein, we initially synthesized the green-fluorescence carbon dots (pm-CDs), and further obtained the composite of pm-CDs@DCDA with a long RTP lifetime of 1.01 s through embedding pm-CDs in dicyandiamide (DCDA). And the bright and long-lived afterglow of pm-CDs@DCDA with 365 nm of UV light excitation was observed by the naked eyes for more than 17 s either emerging as the dry solid or in water. Importantly, the phosphorescence intensity and lifetime of pm-CDs@DCDA were remarkably promoted owing to the intermolecular hydrogen bonds and the rigid environment, hence facilitating the intersystem crossing (ISC) process and restricting the non-radiative transition of triplet excitons. Taking advantage of the superior solid-state luminescence of pm-CDs@DCDA, we further innovatively prepared the white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) with the tunable color temperatures by regulating the mass of pm-CDs@DCDA coated on the chips. This proposed study originally employed DCDA as a matrix to separate and immobilize pm-CDs, which built up a new avenue to improve the RTP property and offered a promising application in WLEDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Long-lived and enhanced phosphorescence
Hydrogen bonds
Space restriction
Composite
White light-emitting diodes

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of WLEDs based on pm-CDs@DCDA composite
Investigation of the long-lived room-temperature phosphorescent mechanism

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Talent and Innovation Project of Beibei District in Chongqing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China ( cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0027 ) and Talent and Innovation Project of Beibei District in Chongqing (2022-11).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the preparation strategy, phosphorescent mechanism and WLEDs application of pm-CDs@DCDA.

(A) TEM, (B) High-resolution TEM and (C) AFM images of pm-CDs; (D) TEM image of pm-CDs@DCDA; (E) Fluorescence spectra of pm-CDs@DCDA; (F) Fluorescence and (G) Room-temperature phosphorescence spectra of DCDA; (H) Fluorescence spectra of pm-CDs; (I) Phosphorescence spectra of pm-CDs@DCDA; (J) CIE coordinates of the photoluminescence for pm-CDs, DCDA and pm-CDs@DCDA.

(A) FT-IR spectra of DCDA, pm-CDs and pm-CDs@DCDA; (B) Raman spectra of pm-CDs; (C) Raman spectra of pm-CDs@DCDA and DCDA; (D)XRD patterns of pm-CDs@DCDA and DCDA; (E) Full XPS survey and (F) C 1 s, (G) N 1 s, (H) O 1 s and (I) P 2p XPS spectra of pm-CDs@DCDA and DCDA.

(A) Illustration of the RTP for pm-CDs@DCDA in dry condition and in water; (B) Visible RTP duration of DCDA and pm-CDs@DCDA by the naked eye in dry condition and in water; (C) Time-resolved phosphorescence spectrum of pm-CDs@DCDA.

(A) Fluorescence and (B) Phosphorescence decay curves of pm-CDs@DCDA with different temperatures; (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of DCDA, pm-CDs and pm-CDs@DCDA; (D) Schematic illustration of the photoluminescence mechanism for pm-CDs@DCDA; (E) DSC trace of pm-CDs@DCDA.

(A) Fluorescence and (B) RTP intensities of pm-CDs@DCDA with continuous excitation; (C) TGA curve of pm-CDs@DCDA; (D), (E) and (F) Emission spectra of pm-CDs@DCDA-based different WLEDs operated at 3 V, inset: photographs of WLEDs under daylight and being working; (G), (H) and (I) CIE coordinates of WLEDs with different CCT and Ra.

head.ce:title: Hydrogen bonds and space restriction promoting long-lived room-temperature phosphorescence and its application for white light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we synthesized the green-fluorescence carbon dots (pm-CDs), and further obtained a long room-temperature phosphorescence lifetime of 1.01 s through embedding pm-CDs in dicyandiamide (DCDA), thus forming the composite of pm-CDs@DCDA. Importantly, the phosphorescence intensity and lifetime of pm-CDs@DCDA were remarkably promoted owing to the intermolecular hydrogen bonds and rigid environment, facilitating the intersystem crossing process and restricting the non-radiative transition of triplet excitons. By virtue of the superior solid-state luminescence of pm-CDs@DCDA, we further prepared the white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) with the tunable color temperatures.

Achieving the long-lived and strong room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) is challengeable but desirable, especially for the enhanced phosphorescence and metal-free nanomaterials. Herein, we initially synthesized the green-fluorescence carbon dots (pm-CDs), and further obtained the composite of pm-CDs@DCDA with a long RTP lifetime of 1.01 s through embedding pm-CDs in dicyandiamide (DCDA). And the bright and long-lived afterglow of pm-CDs@DCDA with 365 nm of UV light excitation was observed by the naked eyes for more than 17 s either emerging as the dry solid or in water. Importantly, the phosphorescence intensity and lifetime of pm-CDs@DCDA were remarkably promoted owing to the intermolecular hydrogen bonds and the rigid environment, hence facilitating the intersystem crossing (ISC) process and restricting the non-radiative transition of triplet excitons. Taking advantage of the superior solid-state luminescence of pm-CDs@DCDA, we further innovatively prepared the white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) with the tunable color temperatures by regulating the mass of pm-CDs@DCDA coated on the chips. This proposed study originally employed DCDA as a matrix to separate and immobilize pm-CDs, which built up a new avenue to improve the RTP property and offered a promising application in WLEDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The exponential growth of room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) nanomaterials in anti-counterfeiting [1], encryption [2–4], nanozymes [5], and imaging [6] has been witnessed over the past decades. However, traditional RTP nanomaterials are limited by the weak electron spin orbit-coupling (SOC) and short lifetime [7,8]. Thus, developing metal-free RTP materials, especially long lifetime RTP nanomaterials that may meet the diverse demands, have been involved extensive research [9–13]. Generally, achieving room-temperature phosphorescence is challenging owing to the weak SOC, large non-radiative rate, and thermal deactivation of triplet excitons. To date, two strategies are basically employed for producing room-temperature phosphorescence [14]. A simple one is through doping heavy atoms, heteroatom, or aromatic carbonyl groups to strengthen SOC, facilitating the intersystem crossing (ISC) process of singlet excitons [15–17]. Immobilizing triplet states is considered as another way which could reduce non-radiative transition via forming intermolecular hydrogen bonds, covalent bonds, cross-linking, or effective space limitation [18–21]. Howbeit, the lifetime of RTP nanomaterials is normally nano-second level and difficult to reach in seconds. Therefore, preparing phosphorescent nanomaterials with a long lifetime via a simple method is still a formidable task.

Carbon dots (CDs), a kind of zero-dimensional (<10 nm) carbon nanomaterial, show a spherical or quasi-spherical core–shell structure [22,23]. Usually, CDs are composed of the carbon core and surface states which could achieve their tunable fluorescence [24–27]. To accomplish an effective RTP, the ISC process should be facilitated and triplet states immobilized [28,29]. In addition, DCDA is a white crystalline compound that may serve as the matrix to provide a rigid and separate environment, and to form intermolecular hydrogen bonds. Therefore, DCDA was selected as the matrix to achieve a long-lived and strong RTP by providing space limitation and stable hydrogen bond networks.

White light-emitting diodes (WLEDs), among various LEDs, are the most widely studied [30–32]. At present, the most common method to obtain CDs-based LEDs is to make chips coated with CDs and UV curing adhesive mixture [33,34]. Nevertheless, the single component of CDs powder generally suffers from fast vibrational dissipation of triplet excitons and aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) extremely limiting their application [35,36]. Additionally, utilizing two or three kinds of materials are usually required for WLEDs fabrication, and achieving the regulation of the correlated color temperature (CCT) and color rendering index (Ra) is also difficult. Towards overcoming this issue, we manufactured WLEDs with different CCT and Ra to meet the requirements of different usage scenarios based on the pm-CDs@DCDA composite.

In this work, we originally prepared the composite of pm-CDs and DCDA (pm-CDs@DCDA) to obtain the long-lived and strong RTP (Fig. 1 ). Specifically, the solvothermal method was employed to synthesize the green fluorescent pm-CDs, and then the composite of pm-CDs@DCDA with red-shifted fluorescence, enhanced and long-lived RTP were prepared with a water bath method. Next, the photoluminescence mechanism of pm-CDs@DCDA was clarified as the intermolecular hydrogen bonds and space limitation. Furthermore, the composite of pm-CDs@DCDA was applied to prepare WLEDs by virtue of their excellent optical property. This strategy offered a simple yet effective way to promote the RTP and significantly prolong RTP lifetime, and provided the evidence for CDs-based composite to obtain WLEDs.

Dicyandiamide (DCDA, C2H4N4), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), secnidazole were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). P-Aminobenzonitrile (C7H6N2), metronidazole (C6H9N3O3) and ornidazole were acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, C2H6OS) was obtained from Khron Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China), and ethanol was purchased from YUEXIANG Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Chongqing, China). In addition, the ultrapure water (UP) (18.25 MΩ.cm) was provided by using ULUPURE ultrapure water system (Sichuan, China). Unless otherwise noted, all the commercial reagents were directly utilized without further purification.

Generally, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan) was introduced to explore the surface functional groups of pm-CDs and pm-CDs@DCDA. Meanwhile, XPS analyses were performed by a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Likewise, a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (Columbia, America) was utilized to obtain the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images at 200 kV, while the AFM image was recorded through Bruker Dimension Icon to analyze the morphology of pm-CDs. Moreover, an FLS1000 Photoluminescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd) was employed to identify the lifetimes and quantum yields (QYs) of pm-CDs and pm-CDs@DCDA. Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) was introduced to record the steady-state fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra. Again, a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) was applied to perform the UV–vis absorption spectra. The lyophilization was conducted by SCIENTZ-12 N (Charlotte, USA) to remove the water of pm-CDs@DCDA. Again, all these characterizations were performed at room temperature.

Besides, the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra and lifetimes at different temperatures (from 77 to 400 K) were achieved on an Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a microsecond flash-lamp (μF900, 100–0.1 Hz) and time gating device (delay time ranges from 0.001 to 9999 ms).

Typically, pm-CDs were synthesized via a solvothermal method. Through a series of ratio optimization, the optimal emission intensity and wavelength were selected for further preparation. 2 mmol of P-Aminobenzonitrile (0.236 g) and 1 mmol of metronidazole (0.171 g) were dissolved in 4 mL of ethanol, and then 1 mL of phosphoric acid was slowly added into this mixture with stirring. Next, the mixture was transferred to 20 mL of Teflon-lined autoclave and then heated at 200 °C for 8 h in a muffle furnace. After the natural cooling-off process, the crude product was obtained. Then, the crude product was first centrifuged (8 min, 10,000 r/min) and filtered through a microfiltration membrane of 0.22 μm to remove the large or agglomerated particles. Finally, the supernatant was collected and stored at 4 °C for the further use.

The pm-CDs@DCDA composite was prepared by the water bath method with stirring. 1.0 g of dicyandiamide (DCDA) was dissolved with 9.5 mL of ultrapure water in a round-bottom flask, and then this mixture was transferred in a water bath kettle at 60 °C for 30 min with stirring (30 r/min). Subsequently, 0.5 mL of pm-CDs was introduced into the round-bottom flask to react at 60 °C for 3 h. Upon cooling, the solid sample was obtained and then washed with ultrapure water several times. Finally, the composite was acquired through freeze-drying as a light-yellow solid.

Likewise, 1 mmol of secnidazole (0.185 g) and 2 mmol of P-Aminobenzonitrile (0.236 g) were successively dissolved in 4 mL of ethanol to synthesize ps-CDs. And then the corresponding composite of ps-CDs@DCDA was obtained based on the same way of preparing pm-CDs@DCDA. Again, 1 mmol of ornidazole (0.220 g) and 2 mmol of P-Aminobenzonitrile (0.236 g) were dissolved in 4 mL of ethanol to prepare po-CDs. And the related composite of po-CDs@DCDA was acquired through the same way of preparing pm-CDs@DCDA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of WLEDs based on pm-CDs@DCDA composite
ce:section.ce:para: First, the blue light emission was emitted by the InGaN chip with 455 nm to verify that the InGaN chips could be used to fabricate WLEDs. Then, the InGaN chips with 455 nm blue light were used as the WLEDs base. The mixture that was composed of pm-CDs@DCDA composite and UV curing adhesive with a mass ratio of 1:1 was employed as color conversion layers. The InGaN chips with the aforementioned mixture covering were heated at 80 °C for 1 h. After that, the WLEDs based on the pm-CDs@DCDA composite were obtained. Besides, the WLEDs with different correlated color temperatures were fabricated by adjusting the mass of the color conversion layer.

Initially, pm-CDs were synthesized through the typical solvothermal method at 200 °C for 8 h, and then pm-CDs@DCDA were prepared on the basis of a stirring water bath approach at 60 °C for 3 h (Figs. S1 and S2). Subsequently, the morphology of pm-CDs and pm-CDs@DCDA was demonstrated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). TEM result of pm-CDs (Fig. 2 A) revealed that pm-CDs were dispersed as spherical particles with the diameters in the inset ranging from 1.57 nm to 5.69 nm (average for 3.17 nm). Moreover, with the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image displayed in Fig. 2B, the obvious lattices in carbon cores of pm-CDs were observed and the lattice spacing was 0.21 nm for (100) facet spacing of graphene [37,38]. Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) image of pm-CDs indicated the average height was 3.91 ± 0.78 nm (Fig. 2C) which approximated its diameters. The little deviation between the height from AFM and the diameter from TEM implicated that the pm-CDs were spherical or spherical-like nanoparticles [39]. As shown in Fig. 2D, the TEM image of pm-CDs@DCDA showed that the composite was non-spherical, which exhibited different morphology compared with the pm-CDs.

Furthermore, pm-CDs showed a green emission at 483 nm with a lifetime of 7.78 ns and a quantum yield of 0.64% under 410 nm excitation (Fig. 2E). As shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescence decay curve of pm-CDs fitted to double-exponential decay and its lifetime was computed by the formula [40,41]:
τ
average
= ∑
α i
τ
i
2
/ ∑
α i
τ i
. In addition, the excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior of pm-CDs was observed (Fig. S4), and it was resulted from the surface-state emissive traps. Meanwhile, the double-exponential decay and excitation-dependent emission revealed that the CDs prepared here showed the multiple-emission centers [42,43]. Meanwhile, DCDA exhibited a blue fluorescence at 405 nm (Fig. 2F) and a pale green phosphorescence at 511 nm (Fig. 2G). As shown in Fig. 2H, the fluorescence of pm-CDs@DCDA obviously red-shifted to yellow-green emission at around 526 nm under 472 nm excitation as well as their phosphorescence emission band at 518 nm under 324 nm excitation (Fig. 2I). Again, the fluorescence quantum yield of pm-CDs@DCDA was measured to be 6.25% as well as the phosphorescence quantum yield of 7.76%. Moreover, the CIE (Commission Internationale de ĺEclairage) coordinates (Table S1) of the photoluminescence spectra of pm-CDs, DCDA, and pm-CDs@DCDA revealed the red-shifting of their emission spectra (Fig. 2J).

To address whether pm-CDs were successfully embedded in the matrix of DCDA, the structure of pm-CDs@DCDA was explored by Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman spectra and X-ray diffraction (XRD), successively. The FT-IR spectra of DCDA, pm-CDs, and pm-CDs@DCDA were displayed in Fig. 3 A. To be specific, there appeared a broad stretching vibration band of OH at 3362 cm−1, asymmetric CH at 2972 cm−1, symmetric CH at 2904 cm−1, keto-carbonyl CO at 1709 cm−1 for pm-CDs [44,45]. DCDA and pm-CDs@DCDA exhibited the similar spectra including NH at 3169 cm−1 and 1568 cm−1, and the typical peaks of terminal alkyne which consisted of CH at 3412 cm−1 and CC at 2181 cm−1 [46]. Besides, the peaks of pm-CDs@DCDA showed wider with the increased intensity, demonstrating that pm-CDs may be embedded in DCDA [47]. The Raman spectrum of pm-CDs exhibited two typical peaks of CDs at 1352 cm−1 (D band) and 1594 cm−1 (G band) (Fig. 3B). The D band resulted from sp3-hybridized carbon which reflected the disorder or defects in the graphitized structure. The G band indicated the sp2 domains corresponding to the graphitized carbon structure [44]. Raman ID /IG ratio (where ID and IG are the Raman intensity of d-band and G-band) was calculated to be 0.89, indicating a large fraction of graphite carbons [48]. In addition, the Raman spectra of pm-CDs@DCDA and DCDA showed similar peaks but different intensities (Fig. 3C), and a new peak at 1726 cm−1 with the strongest intensity emerged in the spectra of pm-CDs@DCDA. Moreover, there emerged the peaks of 2158 cm−1, 933 cm−1, 667 cm−1, 519 cm−1 and 498 cm−1 for both DCDA and pm-CDs@DCDA, illustrating the existence of the interactions between pm-CDs and DCDA. Again, the powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) profile of pm-CDs@DCDA showed four prime peaks at 14.90°, 26.19°, 30.10° and 38.97°, mainly agreeing with the corresponding result for DCDA at 15.05°, 26.11°, 29.85° and 38.07° (Fig. 3D). These findings further confirmed that pm-CDs were embedded in DCDA.

The full XPS spectra of pm-CDs@DCDA showed four typical peaks at 532.19 eV, 399.14 eV, 286.37 eV and 113.48 eV (Fig. 3E), indicating that pm-CDs@DCDA mainly consisted of O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s, P 2p. The relative contents of C, N, O, and P for pm-CDs@DCDA were calculated to be 35.09%, 43.41%, 17.83% and 3.67% (Table S2). Nevertheless, there only emerged two peaks at 398.76 eV and 286.16 eV for the full XPS spectra of DCDA, representing N 1 s and C 1 s, respectively. And the relative contents of C and N for DCDA were obtained as 37.17% and 62.83%. For pm-CDs@DCDA, the C 1 s spectrum showed three gaussian peaks at 288.32 eV, 286.37 eV and 284.70 eV, which corresponded to CN/CO, CN/CO and CC (Fig. 3F). For C 1 s spectrum of DCDA, three peaks at 288.3 eV, 286.17 eV and 284.22 eV were attributed to CN, CN and CC, respectively. And for N 1 s, pm-CDs@DCDA were fitted with two peaks at 399.30 eV and 398.40 eV, which were similar as two peaks at 389.90 eV and 398.00 eV of N 1 s spectrum for DCDA, referring to NH and NC-N (Fig. 3G). Moreover, the O 1 s of pm-CDs@DCDA could be assigned to COC/C-OH (532.44 eV) and CO/PO (530.96 eV) (Fig. 3H), while the P 2p was associated with PN (134.38 eV) and PO/PO (133.48 eV) (Fig. 3I). Taken together, the similarity of XPS C 1 s and N1s for pm-CD@DCDA and DCDA as well as the abundant NH, CN and PO on the surfaces of pm-CD@DCDA, we thus concluded that pm-CDs were successfully embedded in DCDA.

As pm-CDs@DCDA emerged as a composite of pm-CDs and DCDA, we speculated that the new luminescence behavior may derive from the interactions between pm-CDs and DCDA as well as the space limitation from DCDA (Fig. 4 A). To be specific, DCDA provided a rigid environment to stabilize and isolate pm-CDs, thus improving their phosphorescent property both in ambient and water. Simultaneously, the bright afterglow of dry pm-CDs@DCDA reached 17 s by the naked eye and even lasted for 21 s in water (Fig. 4B), which may be attributed to the hydrogen bond networks between H2O and pm-CDs@DCDA. Based on the time-resolved phosphorescence spectrum of pm-CDs@DCDA (Fig. 4C), the intensity dropped to a low level after 10 s, suggesting that this composite showed a relatively slow decay rate and a long lifetime.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the long-lived room-temperature phosphorescent mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To gain insight into the mechanism of the unique photoluminescence, the fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetimes of pm-CDs@DCDA with different temperatures were obtained. As temperatures gradually rose from 77 K to 400 K, their fluorescence lifetimes with a narrow range of 4.12 ns to 4.51 ns were barely affected by temperature (Fig. 5 A, Table S3). However, the phosphorescence lifetime significantly varied from 1.31 s to 0.59 s with the temperature rising (Fig. 5B). Additionally, the RTP lifetime jumped from 7.54 μs of DCDA to 1.01 s of pm-CDs@DCDA (Fig. S5), and the detailed data of lifetimes were displayed in Table S4 and Table S5. According to Fig. S5A, the phosphorescence property of DCDA was proved by the sharply declined intensity with the temperature rising from 77 K to 400 K [49]. Additionally, the phosphorescence decay curves of DCDA fitted to double-exponential decay and their lifetimes were computed at 7.54 μs and 11.53 μs at room temperature and 77 K, separately (Fig. S5B-C, Table S5). Moreover, both the fluorescent and phosphorescent intensity of pm-CDs@DCDA decreased when the temperature increased from 77 K to 400 K (Fig. S6). Taken together, the afterglow emission was phosphorescence instead of thermally activated delay fluorescence, and the RTP lifetime was tremendously improved.

Furthermore, we analyzed the UV–vis absorption spectra of DCDA, pm-CDs, and pm-CDs@DCDA (Fig. 5C). One absorption peak of pm-CDs@DCDA at 257 nm (4.82 eV) corresponded to the absorption band of DCDA at the same position, and the other peak at 288 nm (4.31 eV) approaching the absorption peak of pm-CDs at 284 nm could be ascribed to π-π* transition of the sp2 carbon domains. Also, the wide and weak absorption band in the range of 330 and 500 nm were assigned to the n-π* transition of CN/CO (Fig. S7). For pm-CDs@DCDA, the absorption peak of CC showed the red-shift from 284 nm to 288 nm, while the wide and weak absorption band expanded from 370 nm to 420 nm. This was due to the changed surface states which could trap more photos, thus leading to the red-shift fluorescence of pm-CDs@DCDA. Taken together, the fluorescence of pm-CDs@DCDA originated from π-π* transition of CC and n-π* transition of CN/CO.

For pm-CDs@DCDA, the fluorescence and phosphorescence emissions under 365 nm excitation were 487 nm (2.55 eV) and 550 nm (2.25 eV) (Fig. S8), which agreed with the afterglow observed by the naked eye, and the ΔEST was accordingly calculated to be 0.30 eV (Fig. 5D). For DCDA, its fluorescence emission wavelength barely changed with different excitation wavelengths (Fig. S9A), but its RTP exhibited excitation-dependent emission behavior (Fig. S9B). In addition, pm-CDs@DCDA showed a shoulder peak of RTP around 520 nm (2.38 eV) corresponding to the 516 nm of phosphorescence emission for DCDA under 365 nm excitation. According to the previous reports, long and short emission wavelengths were originated from low-energy surface oxide triplet states and high-energy N-related triplet states, respectively [50]. The phosphorescence lifetime of pm-CDs@DCDA consisted of τ1、τ2、τ3, but there were only two components for DCDA. Specifically, the long emission wavelength (O-related emission center) derived from pm-CDs while there was no oxygen element for DCDA, and the shortest lifetime component which decayed fast was responsible for the yellow afterglow. The longer lifetime components of τ2 and τ3 were ascribed to the improved green phosphorescence of DCDA. Therefore, pm-CDs@DCDA phosphors showed two phosphorescent emission centers with different decay rates and mainly emitted the green RTP. Thus, the emission peak of 550 nm was resulted from the RTP of pm-CDs, while the band at 520 nm was originated from the enhanced RTP of DCDA.

To deeply understand the photoluminescence mechanism, differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) was further employed to investigate the interactions between pm-CDs and DCDA (Fig. 5E). The DSC trace showed an endothermic peak at 163.3 °C of the heating phase, which was ascribed to the broken of intermolecular hydrogen bonds [51]. Particularly, DCDA abounded with NH bonds could easily form the intermolecular hydrogen bonds with the CO, PO functional groups of pm-CDs. The hydrogen bonds network induced the composite more stable and suppressed the nonradiative transition to improve the phosphorescence lifetime and intensity (Fig. S10) [52,53]. Meanwhile, DCDA also provided a rigid environment to protect the entirety against oxygen and water and restrict the movement of molecules, thus reducing the vibration and rotation of the surface functional groups and suppressing the nonradiative transition, which eventually resulted in the RTP generation of pm-CDs [8,11]. Overall, the long-lived phosphorescence derived from the intermolecular hydrogen bonds the immobilization of the DCDA matrix.

To identify the effect of different functional groups of nitroimidazoles on CDs and CDs@DCDA composite, ornidazole and secnidazole with only one different functional group were introduced compared with metronidazole (Fig. S11A-B). The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of po-CDs and ps-CDs were similar to that of pm-CDs (Fig. S11C-D), and their composite po-CDs@DCDA and ps-CDs@DCDA (Fig. S11E-F) almost exhibited the same absorption and emission spectra compared with that of pm-CDs@DCDA. Consequently, the nitroimidazoles also showed the possibility to prepare this type of CDs as well as their composites showing similar optical properties.

Moreover, the fluorescent and phosphorescent stability were investigated, confirming that the composite of pm-CDs@DCDA showed the potential to prepare WLEDs. Their luminescence intensity was maintained with scarce loss, which could be regarded as a promising candidate for manufacturing WLEDs (Fig. 6 A-B). In addition, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) showed that the degradation started when the temperature rose to 229.9 °C, indicating the favorable thermostability of pm-CDs@DCDA (Fig. 6C) [54]. Based on the optimal excitation wavelength of pm-CDs@DCDA, 455 nm of InGaN chip was selected as the excitation light source (Fig. S12), and the WLEDs with different CCT were obtained through adjusting the mass of the mixture of UV curing adhesive and pm-CDs@DCDA (1:1). Specifically, with increasing mass of the mixture covered on the InGaN chips, the emission peak red-shifted and the intensity was gradually enhanced while CCT obviously declined (Fig. 6D-F). The CIE coordinates of cold-white light were (0.30, 0.30), and CCT was 7822 K with a color rendering index (Ra) of 89.3 (Fig. 6G). With the amount of the mixture increasing, the CCT coordinates shifted toward low temperature along the Planckian locus, and the pure-white light with CIE coordinates of (0.33, 0.34), color temperature of 5612 K, and Ra of 88.7 was obtained (Fig. 6H). Importantly, warm-white WLEDs with lower CCT of 4058 K, CIE coordinates of (0.38, 0.38), and Ra of 80.5 was achieved (Fig. 6I), which could meet the indoor illumination standard. Thus, through increasing the mass of pm-CDs@DCDA, more blue light from 455 nm of InGaN chips was reabsorbed, leading to the emission red-shifted and distinct growth in emission intensity [55,56]. Furthermore, the proportion of blue emission was in accordance with CCT, suggesting that WLEDs with different CCT and Ra could be acquired through adjusting the contents of pm-CDs@DCDA.

In summary, the composite of pm-CDs@DCDA was originally acquired by embedding pm-CDs in DCDA. In particular, pm-CDs@DCDA exhibited a long-lived RTP lifetime of 1.01 s, which was originated from the rigid environment provided by DCDA and the stabilization of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between pm-CDs and DCDA. Significantly, the proposed composite showed the improved phosphorescence not only in the solid state but also in aqueous condition, which may extend their applications rather than just serve as the solid-state phosphorescent nanomaterials [35,57]. Additionally, pm-CDs@DCDA was innovatively applied to prepare WLEDs with different color temperature, suggesting their promising prospect in the field of WLEDs lighting [31]. Overall, this work provides a facile and effective way to improve the RTP property through introducing DCDA as a matrix and shows the great potential for the optical device manufacturing based on the composite raised here.

Miao Fu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Liuquan Lin: Investigation, Methodology, Software. Xin Wang: Writing – review & editing. Xiaoming Yang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China (cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0027) and Talent and Innovation Project of Beibei District in Chongqing (2022-11).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.02.040.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
